critic_raw:
FamNet's few-shot counting uses a test-time adaptation strategy that updates the model using the exemplar objects provided alongside the query image. This adaptation is performed at test time using the same exemplars that are used to define the object category to count — the model sees the target exemplars, adapts its parameters to those exemplars, and then makes predictions using those adapted parameters. This creates a form of test-time information leakage: the model is not operating as a pure feed-forward inference engine but is performing gradient-based optimization using test-time target exemplars. If the evaluation protocol does not rigorously hold out the test exemplars until after the initial (pre-adaptation) prediction is made, the comparison between FamNet with and without adaptation is conflated with the quality of the exemplars rather than the adaptation mechanism.

The FSC-147 dataset spans 147 visual categories, but the 147-category split may have class imbalance in terms of object count distributions, object scales, and contextual complexity. Few-shot counting performance is heavily dependent on exemplar quality and scale match between exemplars and countable objects in the query image. If certain categories in the test split systematically provide better exemplar-object scale alignment, the reported mean absolute error (MAE) aggregates over these conditions and hides category-level variance. Categories where exemplar scale mismatches are common may show much higher errors than the aggregate suggests.

The comparison against 'object detectors and few-shot counting approaches' requires that all compared methods use the same number of exemplars (same K) and the same test-time computation budget. Object detectors that are fine-tuned on the exemplar category have seen more information than FamNet during the counting operation. If the comparison does not equalize exemplar usage, FamNet's advantage over object detectors may simply reflect that object detectors are not given sufficient examples to specialize effectively at test time.

The claim that FamNet reduces error by increasing the number of exemplars implies a monotone improvement curve, but this may only hold up to a saturation point determined by the model's memory capacity for exemplar features. Without reporting the full exemplar-count vs. error curve (not just selective data points), the claim cannot be evaluated, and the saturation regime — where adding exemplars does not help — reveals the model's representational bottleneck.
all_issues_raised: Test-time adaptation using the same exemplars that define the counting category creates information leakage: the model optimizes its parameters using the very objects it then uses to count, and if evaluation does not separate pre- and post-adaptation predictions, the adaptation benefit is confounded with exemplar quality.

FSC-147 category-level variance in exemplar scale alignment is hidden by aggregate MAE; categories with systematic exemplar-object scale mismatch likely show substantially higher errors that the mean metric does not expose.

Comparison against object detectors and few-shot methods requires equalizing exemplar usage and test-time computation; detectors not given sufficient exemplars to specialize are disadvantaged by the comparison protocol, inflating FamNet's apparent advantage.

The 'error reduction with more exemplars' claim requires a full exemplar-count vs. error curve to identify the saturation point; reporting only selective data points obscures the representational bottleneck where additional exemplars provide no benefit.
all_issues_adjudicated: Test-time adaptation using the same exemplars that define the counting category creates information leakage: the model optimizes its parameters using the very objects it then uses to count, and if evaluation does not separate pre- and post-adaptation predictions, the adaptation benefit is confounded with exemplar quality.

FSC-147 category-level variance in exemplar scale alignment is hidden by aggregate MAE; categories with systematic exemplar-object scale mismatch likely show substantially higher errors that the mean metric does not expose.

Comparison against object detectors and few-shot methods requires equalizing exemplar usage and test-time computation; detectors not given sufficient exemplars to specialize are disadvantaged by the comparison protocol, inflating FamNet's apparent advantage.

The 'error reduction with more exemplars' claim requires a full exemplar-count vs. error curve to identify the saturation point; reporting only selective data points obscures the representational bottleneck where additional exemplars provide no benefit.
